items.q: If you stop training, what happens to your fitness gains?
items.random_wrong: They keep improving on their own.
items.why: Without continued training the body de-adapts, so gains are gradually lost.

items.correct: Gradually increasing the training demands over time.
items.misconception: Training as hard as possible in every single session.
items.random_wrong: Keeping the workload exactly the same each week.
items.why: Improvement needs steadily rising demands, but increases must be gradual to avoid injury.

items.q: Why must a PEP follow the specificity principle?
items.correct: So training matches the muscles and energy systems the activity actually uses.
items.misconception: So every athlete does the identical programme.
items.random_wrong: So you only ever do cardio.
items.why: Training adaptations are specific, so a programme should target the demands of the chosen sport.

items.q: What do the letters FITT stand for?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Frequency, Intensity, Time, Type. “Fitness, Intensity, Time, Type” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Gradually increasing training demands over time
items.misconception: Training the same way every week
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gradually increasing training demands over time. “Training the same way every week” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Match the demands of your activity
items.why: It's Match the demands of your activity. “Be random” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Reversibility describes what happens when you:
items.why: Correct answer: Stop training and lose fitness. “Train harder” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Using the formula 220 minus age, what is the estimated maximum heart rate of a 20-year-old?
items.why: Exact answer: 200 bpm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
